data.title: Huron raises 2025 RBR outlook to $1.68B and EPS target to $7.70 while advancing commercial and healthcare acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huron Consulting Group Inc. (HURN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO C. Mark Hussey reported that "revenues before reimbursable expenses or RBR in the quarter grew 8% over the second quarter of 2024, including organic RBR growth across all 3 operating segments. RBR in the 2025 was a record high for our business." Hussey highlighted strong demand in core markets as clients adapt to regulatory and macroeconomic pressures, driving confidence in "our outlook for continued growth in 2025 as reflected in our updated annual guidance."
  • Hussey detailed that the Healthcare segment saw RBR growth of 4% year-over-year, excluding divested student education, with a 6% increase. The sector faces "anticipated reduction to the federal spending on health care" from recent legislation, prompting urgent client action on margin declines and financial pressures. Hussey noted the acquisition of Eclipse Insights, which "strengthens Huron's mid-revenue cycle expertise, enhancing our ability to support providers across the full revenue cycle continuum."
  • The Education segment achieved 5% RBR growth, with record sales conversion and robust demand for strategy, operations, and digital offerings. Hussey cited ongoing regulatory uncertainty and heightened focus on optimizing research and fundraising strategies.
  • Commercial segment RBR rose 28%, driven by the AXIA acquisition and strong digital demand. Excluding AXIA, digital RBR grew 23%. Hussey announced the acquisition of Treliant, expanding risk management and compliance capabilities for financial services clients.
  • Hussey stated, "Inclusive of our recent acquisitions, today, we're increasing our RBR guidance to a range of $1.64 billion to $1.68 billion... Maintaining our adjusted EBITDA margin guidance range of 14.0% to 14.5% of RBR and increasing our adjusted non-GAAP EPS guidance to a range of $7.30 to $7.70, which represents an increase of 16% at the midpoint as compared to full year 2024."
  • CFO John D. Kelly remarked, "RBR for the second quarter of 2025 was $402.5 million, up 8.3% from $371.7 million in the same quarter of 2024. Organic RBR... was 4.2% over the prior year quarter, driven by growth across all 3 operating segments."

Outlook

  • Management raised annual RBR guidance to $1.64 billion to $1.68 billion and adjusted non-GAAP EPS guidance to $7.30 to $7.70, with adjusted EBITDA margin targeted at 14% to 14.5% of RBR.
  • Healthcare revenue is now projected to achieve upper single-digit percentage growth with operating margins of 28% to 30% for the year.
  • Education revenue growth is expected in the mid- to upper single-digit range, with operating margins of 23% to 25%.
  • Commercial segment growth is forecast in the mid-20% range, with operating margins of 18% to 20%.
  • The impact of Eclipse and Treliant acquisitions is expected to be neutral to adjusted EPS in 2025 but accretive in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q2 2025 was $19.4 million or $1.09 per diluted share, compared to $37.5 million or $2.03 in Q2 2024. The quarter included an $8.2 million noncash impairment charge; Q2 2024 included an $11.1 million litigation settlement gain.
  • Adjusted EBITDA reached $60.6 million, or 15.1% of RBR, compared to $55.7 million, or 15% of RBR, in Q2 2024.
  • Healthcare segment RBR was $197.8 million, Education $129.3 million, and Commercial $75.4 million, each with year-over-year growth. Commercial segment growth was boosted by AXIA’s $12.3 million contribution.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $73.7 million, with cash flow from operations at $80 million. Total debt was $657.8 million with cash of $61 million at quarter-end.
  • The company repurchased $61 million in shares during the quarter and amended its credit facility to increase borrowing capacity to $1.1 billion.

Q&A

  • Andrew Owen Nicholas, William Blair: Asked about visibility post-legislation. CEO Hussey said, "It's like, okay, well, we're no longer waiting to see what happens. We now know what's going to happen. And so now we're in a position to start to make some firmer decisions without that uncertainty."
  • Nicholas followed up on healthcare digital sales delays. CFO Kelly responded, "our guidance for the year is not contingent on any assumptions related to the pace of conversion on the digital side of the business... underlying projects, eventually, they need to get done."
  • Tobey O'Brien Sommer, Truist Securities: Inquired about pipeline conversion delays. CEO Hussey explained, "we've seen just the increase of our clients whose C-suites are focused on driving financial stability... not that they've gone away. It's just they have more pressing needs in the context of where we are."
  • Sommer asked about headcount growth; CFO Kelly attributed it to managed services and the Eclipse acquisition, "in the neighborhood of 40 new team members."
  • Sommer sought acquisition progress details. CEO Hussey said, "we have -- I would expect there might be maybe 1 or 2" more tuck-in deals possible by year end.
  • William Sutherland, Benchmark Company: Asked about utilization rates. CFO Kelly replied, "it's closer to the top of the range in terms of utilization... certain teams that have even higher utilization that blend into that up into the 80% range."
  • Sutherland queried education sales momentum. CFO Kelly pointed to "strategy and operations team... investments in technology or helping our clients right now work through their strategy through what's a fairly disruptive environment."
  • Sutherland asked about healthcare consolidation. CEO Hussey noted, "helping them on the post-merger integration work as well."
  • Kevin Mark Steinke, Barrington: Requested examples on healthcare cost management. CEO Hussey described comprehensive performance improvement offerings "from revenue cycle to supply chain work to workforce set up to the clinical operations."
  • Steinke asked about the Treliant acquisition. CEO Hussey explained, "financial services has been the #1 or #2 industry segment that we've had... Treliant are highly complementary to what they do."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, seeking clarity on healthcare digital delays, acquisition strategy, and the impact of legislative changes. Questions were direct but did not express skepticism or overt concern.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing clarity from legislation and confidence in guidance. CEO Hussey and CFO Kelly were precise about growth drivers and integration of acquisitions. Confidence was underlined with phrases such as "we believe we will continue to be well positioned" and "we feel very well positioned to help them on the performance improvement side."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains steady and constructive, with more emphasis on record sales conversion and the positive impact of recent acquisitions. Analyst tone was consistent with the prior quarter, remaining primarily information-seeking and constructive.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was increased from the prior range of $1.58 billion to $1.66 billion RBR and $6.80 to $7.50 adjusted EPS, now raised to $1.64 billion to $1.68 billion and $7.30 to $7.70 adjusted EPS, respectively.
  • Strategic focus remains on organic growth, complemented by targeted acquisitions, with recent deals (Eclipse Insights, Treliant) enhancing capabilities in healthcare and commercial sectors.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward execution risks related to legislative clarity, digital sales conversion, and acquisition integration, whereas the previous quarter focused more on pipeline strength and headcount alignment.
  • Management sentiment and confidence levels remained strong, with more specifics shared around acquisition integration and segment growth expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted risks from healthcare legislation (One Big Beautiful Bill Act), which is expected to reduce federal healthcare spending by over $1 trillion over the next decade and increase the uninsured population by up to 10 million, elevating uncompensated care costs for hospitals.
  • The company is responding by expanding performance improvement and digital offerings, and by acquiring specialized firms to address client needs in a constrained funding environment.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the pace of digital project sales and the sustainability of current utilization and headcount growth.

Final Takeaway

Huron's leadership reiterated confidence in its growth strategy, supported by robust demand across core segments, record sales conversions, and enhanced capabilities through strategic acquisitions. The company raised its full-year RBR and EPS guidance, reflecting improved visibility and execution despite macroeconomic and regulatory challenges, while emphasizing the ongoing opportunities created by market disruption and client needs.
